Yes, it's one of the biggest one 
). 
I have mixed feelings, I think that of all the exchanges out there it is probably the best one and the most 
above board one (maybe together with Gemini). 
The mixed feelings part is about the future, you can argue that to justify the valuation ($8bn) what has to 
happen is a big uptick in usage. 
This uptick should either happen from institutional investors or consumer. The negative view on this goes as 
follows: 
their institutional platform is (again) one of the best ones today but it's not really hard to replicate. On the 
consumer side, if usage does take up then banks are going to get into it and it's unclear whether people will 
still buy from Coinbase. As an example Citi declared that they are going to issue the equivalent of ADR for 
bitcoin. 
The positive narrative says that institutions or consumers usage will spike and Coinbase will get at least some 
of that and then they could either go public or be a quick acquisition for a bank that wants to get into crypto 
but has nothing built yet. 
This is a long way of saying that I'm not concerned too much about regulation (if anything I think it's a 
positive for Coinbase) but I'm concerned about price. I think at $ 1bn it would be a very good buy, at $8bn 
I'm not entirely sure. 
That said, Tiger is leading the round so maybe there are good provisions in the deals that take the above into 
account. As far as I know the business generates a lot of cash flow and profit, so it might be that tiger will 
have something

evidence of the Origins project, its contributions, and its mission. As it has been handled, the process has 
been unfair to Origins participants, donors, and members of the general public, and goes against what 
should be the University's commitment to knowledge and education. 
I respectfully request that: 
(a) The Origins web site, with all its resources and links, be restored for the benefit of those individuals 
who use the site, and in honor of all the people who contributed time and scholarship to the program. 
(b) Those who have donated to Origins be informed that their funds are now being used to support the 
Interplanetary Initiative and that they be given the option of moving those funds to other programs that 
may better reflect their interests. 
(c) Many people on the Origins emailing list have been writing to me out of confusion, as they are now 
receiving Interplanetary Initiative emails. Once again, I think it would be a ethical to send a mailing to 
all those on the Origins mailing list explaining that since Origins is now terminated, they will be 
receiving emails and announcements from the Interplanetary Initiative unless they choose to opt out of 
receiving such emails, and then giving them an option to opt out. 
(d) The Origins Postdoctoral Prize Lectureship was funded by a donor who wanted to support the Origins 
mission. While I am very happy that the individual who the Origins Project committee and I selected for 
the Origins Postdoctoral Prize in February will in fact be giving a lecture this fall, this lecture should be 
called the Origins Postdoctoral Prize Lecture, because that is what it is, and that was what the award was 
given for. If you decide to raise and/or devote funds from your initiative for a new lectureship after this, 
then it would be appropriate to rename future lectures. 
Thanks for your attention to these concerns.

It is a very interesting topic. 
Basically there are two types: 
- hard-pegged IOU-based ones (like Tether or Gemini Dollar) where you deposit $1 in the bank and 
get 1 stablecoin; 
- soft-pegged algorithmic ones (essentially loans against a cryptocurrency with interesting incentive 
structures hups://medium.com/®james 3093/the-dai-stablecoin-is-a-game-changer-for-ethereum-and-
the-entire-cryptocurrency-ecosystem-13fb412dle75 ) 
EFTA01021087

The algorithmic ones will probably be wiped out if there is a flash crash or other black swan event. 
But so far the peg has been relatively close to 1. 
The most popular IOU-based one (Tether) is widely assumed to be created for evading banking 
blockade for KYC/AML non-compliant exchanges. On the other hand, Gemini is doing everything by 
the book (they were the first NYDFS-compliant Zcash exchange, for example) so not competing for 
random customers. There are USD, Tether, and Gemini Dollar trading pairs and the peg is very close 
to 1 in practice. 
Madars

Hello Mr. Gordon, 
Attached is our proposal for the asphalt paving improvements at Zorro Ranch. Our 
schedule would be to start on August 13, 2018 and complete the work in eight weeks. Our 
price is based on purchasing the asphalt mix from Mountain States Constructors in 
Moriarty, NM. They have a hot mix asphalt plant set up there to take care of the paving 
project on NM 41 by the ranch. However, they can not guarantee that the plant will still be 
there after October, so it is imperative to get started on the project soon. The cost of asphalt 
cement has been rising lately and to bring in material from our regular supplier in 
Albuquerque would add approximately $ 40,000.00 to the cost. Our payment terms are as 
follows: 40% down payment upon mobilization. Monthly pay requests submitted on the

Hi Jeffrey 
I did a preliminary call with the CEO (Isaiah Cox). 
• Company looking to raise $12MM which should get them through final FAA certification and enable the first 
commercial aircraft installation in 2019. 
• The company will provide the equipment free of charge (including installation) to the airlines. Cost to the company of 
$200K. In return they take half of the savings generated by having the device installed. 
• Installation takes 8-12 hours per plane. Teams will travel around the world for installations. 
• See big demand from Middle East airlines (not needing to keep the engines running and therefore less damage from 
dust). Currently order book over 1000. 
• Savings determined by: 
o Push back fee — agreed and known 
o Pre-agree fuel savings 
o Time saved on ground by turning around the plane more quickly and getting back into the air- measured as 
$/minute saved 
• Wheel Tug would keep half these savings 
• Deloitte study (attached) gave a wide range of $440 - $3300 savings per flight per plane 
• They offered a deal where you get 10% of revenue until you get a multiple of your investment. How about asking for 
10% of revenue until we get 5x our investment back plus warrants at today's valuation (currently at $1.3BIn). Also 
would ask for liquidation priority over all existing equity holders. 
• They assume 1720 flights per year per plane and targeting an installed base of 1700 planes by 2022. 
• Based on a Deloitte study of potential savings combined with the company's installation projections, below is a table 
showing the 10% of Wheel Tug's revenue that w

Any chance we can get your pilot on a call with the CEO to verify these assumptions? 
Summary: The WheelTug incorporates the elements of an electric motor, such as the stator and the rotor, inside the hub of 
each of the 737's twin nose wheels. Instead of a battery, these motors draw current directly from the auxiliary power-unit, a 
small generator in the rear of an airliner that is used on the ground to run its electrical services. For a long taxi, they can also 
take power from the generators driven by the aircraft's jet engines

Final company meeting was with Brightseed, an intro from Bart Swanson at Horizon Ventures. They have created a 
computational model, Forager, that mines their proprietary data from the world's plants and human biology, 
discovering natural compounds that solve unmet human problems. In a nutshell, they are able to find these 
compounds, then licence them for manufacture to then be incorporated into everyday foods to enhance people's 
health, naturally. As you can imagine, they have had a lot of inbound from the major US and European food 
companies that are always up against regulators and politicians demanding that their foods are healthier/ not so full 
of sugar/ have more nutrients etc etc. Their model has currently identified one indication, and they are looking to 
raise a Series A in Q3 '19 (if not before —they have had some pre-emptive inbound) of around $15-20m1n to enable 
them to explore a second indication. For me, what they are doing is super interesting, although I found the team a 
bit flat and not particularly inspiring. That may be to do with the fact that they are currently heavily scientist-centric, 
and will remain so for a while, but still didn't really pick up the energ
